title: QUIC client-side successor 1-RTT Key Phase install architecture
purpose: Describe how the managed client/runtime path retains the current 1-RTT application traffic secrets long enough to derive successor packet-protection material on the first observed 0->1 Key Phase transition, installs the new open/protect pair into the existing bridge-state fields, and switches outbound 1-RTT packets to the installed Key Phase bit without introducing a general TLS KeyUpdate engine.
risks: If the key schedule still drops the current 1-RTT application traffic secrets after initial publication, the successor pair cannot be derived at the first observed transition.
If the outbound short-header formatter ignores the installed Key Phase bit, the send path and bridge-state view can diverge after installation.
If the slice expands into a general TLS KeyUpdate engine or repeated successor derivation, it overclaims the current boundary instead of staying on the narrow client-side install seam.
design_summary: The key schedule keeps the client's active 1-RTT application traffic secrets available after initial publication. When the runtime observes the first peer 0->1 Key Phase transition on the active short-header path, the successor open/protect pair is derived from the retained secrets and published through the existing bridge-state update path. The bridge state installs the new pair and flips its key-phase view to 1, and the short-header formatter then reads the installed phase bit for subsequent outbound 1-RTT packets. Establishing runtimes remain unchanged before handshake confirmation, and the slice stops at the first successor install instead of building a general RFC 9001 key-update engine.
alternatives_considered: Keep the observation-only boundary from REQ-QUIC-CRT-0144. Rejected because the next bounded slice needs a real successor-install seam, not another marker-only update.
Publish a public key-update API. Rejected because the slice must remain internal, managed-client only, and closed to public key-update promises.
edge_cases_and_constraints: The first observed 0->1 transition is the only installation trigger.
Duplicate same-phase packets do not re-trigger installation.
Establishing runtimes remain unchanged before handshake confirmation.
The slice does not add TLS KeyUpdate support, transfer enablement, retry enablement, public API widening, or any public key-update promise.
